name: Cranelift
summary: Bytecode-Alliance compiler backend (originally Mozilla for Wasmtime) prioritizing compile speed over peak code quality. ISLE-driven instruction selection, regalloc2 SSA-aware allocator, e-graph mid-end. ~10× faster than LLVM at modestly lower code quality. Targets x86-64, aarch64, s390x, riscv64.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Cranelift is a compiler backend developed by the Bytecode Alliance (originally by Mozilla for Wasmtime). Unlike LLVM, Cranelift prioritizes compilation speed while maintaining reasonable code quality — making it ideal for JIT and baseline compilation tiers.

Production users: Wasmtime (WebAssembly), `rustc_codegen_cranelift` (alternative Rust backend for debug builds, 2–4× faster compile than LLVM), and several other projects. Targets x86-64, aarch64, s390x, and riscv64.

sections.title: CLIF — Cranelift IR
sections.content:
Cranelift's IR (CLIF) is SSA-based but designed for compile speed rather than peak code quality. It uses dense integer indices instead of pointers (similar to V8 Turboshaft), keeps types minimal (`i8`/`i16`/`i32`/`i64`/`f32`/`f64`/vector + references), and is deliberately lower-level than LLVM IR — no target-independent bitcasts, fewer implicit conversions, fewer node kinds.

CLIF uses block parameters rather than phi nodes for control-flow merges: each block declares parameters, and branch instructions pass arguments. This is cleaner for many transformations because branch instructions name their arguments at the call site rather than at the destination.

sections.title: Fit for JIT and tiered compilation
sections.content:
Cranelift compiles ~10× faster than LLVM at modestly lower code quality. This makes it attractive where code generation latency matters: JITs (Wasmtime is the canonical case), runtime specialization, debug-build compilation, and tooling scenarios that cannot afford heavyweight compilation on the critical path.

`rustc_codegen_cranelift` is the production existence proof for the LLVM-as-AOT-backend / Cranelift-as-debug-backend split: Rust developers get faster `cargo build` cycles when iterating on debug builds, and the same Rust toolchain switches to LLVM for release builds where peak performance matters.

sections.title: ISLE and regalloc2
sections.content:
Two key innovations distinguish Cranelift architecturally:

**ISLE** (Instruction Selection Lowering Expressions) is a statically-typed term-rewriting DSL for instruction selection rules. Rules are written declaratively and compiled to Rust match trees; the ISLE compiler merges rules into a decision tree, sharing matching work where possible. This replaces hand-written C++ lowering code with rules that are easier to verify, fuzz, and maintain.

**regalloc2** (Chris Fallin) is the register allocator: combines aspects of linear scan with SSA-aware splitting. Status (as of 2026-04): provides ~20% faster compilation than its predecessor while improving code quality 10–20% on register-pressure-heavy benchmarks. Operates on SSA form directly, splits live ranges at block boundaries, and uses parallel move resolution.

sections.title: Optimization scope and philosophy
sections.content:
Cranelift prioritizes practical compile speed and adequate optimization over maximal sophistication. The mid-end uses an *aegraph* (acyclic e-graph): rewrite rules — strength reduction, algebraic identities, GVN — express as ISLE patterns matching against the aegraph. This gives some of the phase-ordering benefits of full equality saturation while keeping extraction tractable.

The philosophy: 50–70% of LLVM's peak performance for 10× faster compilation, with predictable behavior and a hackable codebase. This makes Cranelift a useful reference backend when predictability, implementation size, or runtime responsiveness matter more than the deepest optimization repertoire.

sections.title: Research comparison points
sections.content:
Cranelift offers a useful middle point in the lightweight-backend design space:

- **More structured than copy-and-patch**: full SSA optimization, block-level GVN, peephole rewrites via ISLE.
- **Lighter and faster than LLVM**: ~10× compile-speed advantage, hackable C-like codebase.
- **More mature than QBE**: production at Wasmtime scale; multi-target codegen including AArch64, RISC-V, s390x.
- **More principled than DynASM-style macroassemblers**: real SSA optimization rather than pushing register allocation onto the human.

For any new language picking a backend, the question is which point on this spectrum fits the workload — Cranelift is the modern default for compile-time-sensitive workloads where SSA-level optimization is wanted.
